teks:
From a Single Idea to Image, Story, and Video
A creative experience to discover how an idea can be developed into an artwork with the help of AI.
Grade
5th Grade
Participant
1 Participant
Duration
45–60 Minutes
Format
Private Home Preview

Every object or surface that is not specifically used for the authorized text must contain zero typography: no words, no letters, no numbers, no symbols resembling writing, and no pseudo-text. Keep book covers, notebook pages, seed packets, tools, screens, buildings, clothing, and background signs blank unless an authorized line is explicitly placed there.

Create a premium cinematic 16:9 opening image for an educational 3D animated presentation. Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as mandatory references. Preserve the robot's exact rounded pearl-white helmet, dark navy face screen, cyan oval eyes, cyan chest core, violet accents, compact child-friendly proportions, hands, feet, and materials. Preserve the exact garden geography, cream two-story school, blue-framed windows, central tree, circular wooden bench, pale stone paths, metal fence, arched entrance, planting beds, irrigation pipes, gardening-tool board, flowers, vegetables, and bright-morning atmosphere.

Show a wide hero view from just outside the garden entrance. Little Robot stands beside the open arched gate and looks into the garden with a curious, welcoming pose, one hand gently presenting the pathway. Build all presentation copy into a coordinated set of real architectural objects at the entrance: a premium teak title sign, an inset stone description plaque, and four glazed-ceramic fact plaques. Every object must have believable thickness, grain or glaze, mounting hardware, contact shadows, perspective, and weather-resistant finish. The lettering must be carved, engraved, or fired into its host material and share the same sunlight; it must never look digitally pasted on. Add a small trail of tangible creative objects along the path—a closed sketchbook, a printed image card, a bound storybook, and a compact video camera—arranged naturally as visual storytelling props without labels.

Composition: cinematic wide establishing shot, child-height viewpoint, 28–35 mm lens feeling, strong depth, clear foreground gate, robot in the middle ground, school and garden behind, warm sunlight from upper right, soft blue ambient light, polished family-friendly 3D animation, clean visual hierarchy, safe margins, high resolution.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the title on the mounted teak entrance sign, the descriptive sentence on an inset stone plaque below it, and the four label-value pairs on four separate glazed-ceramic plaques attached to the gate columns.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating text, no subtitles, no lower thirds, no title overlay, no holographic words, no speech bubbles, no captions, no UI, no watermark, no logo, no extra text, no misspelled letters, no detached sign, no flat pasted typography, no redesigned robot, no additional robots, no children or adults, no moved garden landmarks, no weapons, no extra limbs, no deformed hands, no cropped title board, no night scene, no photorealistic live-action style.

teks:
What Will We Do?
In this preview session, the child will create a mini project from a simple idea and see how it can be developed into a video.
01
Idea
Determining characters, settings, problems, and solutions.
02
Story
Structuring the initial plot, action, and resolution.
03
Visual AI
Generating images with AI based on the child's idea.
04
Short Video
Seeing how images can be animated or compiled into a video.
The child remains the owner of the idea.
AI is used as a tool to bring their creativity to life.

Create a polished cinematic 16:9 educational 3D animation image that explains a four-step creative journey inside the established School Garden. Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as mandatory references. Keep the Little Robot and every major garden landmark exactly consistent with those references.

Stage the camera in a medium-wide view looking down the pale stone pathway from the entrance toward the central tree. Little Robot is walking along the path with an enthusiastic but natural learning pose, carrying a small closed sketchbook under one arm. Integrate all presentation copy into permanent physical garden wayfinding: one mounted directory at the path entrance, four numbered glazed-ceramic station plaques embedded beside the path, and one closing brass plaque fixed to the circular bench. Use harmonious cyan, violet, teal, and warm-gold inlays. Letters must be recessed or fired into their host materials, aligned to perspective, and affected by sunlight and contact shadows. Connect the four stations using a thin physical brass line embedded in the stone path, never a floating arrow.

Add tangible storytelling props near each step without clutter: a pencil and seed-shaped sketch token near the first station, a small printed picture card near the second, a bound miniature storybook near the third, and a compact camera on a garden bench near the fourth. All objects must rest on real surfaces with correct scale and shadows. The garden remains bright, colorful, safe, and cheerful.

Composition: readable left-to-right or foreground-to-background progression, 35 mm cinematic lens, gentle leading lines, robot as the emotional anchor, central tree and school building recognizable, warm bright-morning lighting, subtle depth of field, clean high-end animated feature style.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the title and introductory sentence on the main garden directory; place each numbered step, its title, and its description on one of four physical station plaques; place the two closing statements on a brass plaque fixed to the circular bench.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating words, no subtitle strip, no infographic overlay, no hologram, no speech bubble, no UI cards, no arrows floating in the air, no text in the sky, no labels detached from objects, no random letters, no extra text, no misspelling, no duplicated robot, no changed robot anatomy, no moved architecture, no crowded props, no children, no watermark, no logo, no cropped pathway text, no extreme top-down angle that makes the robot unreadable.

<AUTHORIZED_TEXT>
AI is a Tool, Not a Replacement for the Child's Ideas
The Child Decides
story ideas;
character choices;
colors and moods;
storyline;
the final result to use;
changes to be made.
AI Assists By
generating visual alternatives;
offering variations;
speeding up exploration;
helping to visualize instructions;
revealing new possibilities.
Child Thinks
Child Gives Instructions
AI Assists
Child Chooses
Child Refines
Child Presents
Technology helps the process. The child still directs the artwork.
</AUTHORIZED_TEXT>
Every object or surface that is not specifically used for the authorized text must contain zero typography: no words, no letters, no numbers, no symbols resembling writing, and no pseudo-text. Keep book covers, notebook pages, seed packets, tools, screens, buildings, clothing, and background signs blank unless an authorized line is explicitly placed there.

Create a premium 16:9 cinematic 3D animated image expressing that AI is a helpful tool while creative direction remains human-led. Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as mandatory references. Reproduce the exact robot identity and the exact gardening-tool area from the school garden.

Place Little Robot at the established wooden gardening workbench beside the real tool board. The robot is using ordinary garden tools to repair a small planter while an open sketchbook on the bench shows a simple hand-drawn plan of the planter. Its posture should communicate assistance, focus, patience, and service—not authority. Use tangible visual metaphors: a rounded wrench, pencil, ruler, seed packets, and watering can are organized neatly as equally useful tools. No tool floats.

Build a believable physical teaching installation from the material language of the garden: fasten a framed two-column brushed stainless-steel and glass board to the wooden tool wall using visible rounded bolts, embed six process markers into the path, and attach a closing plaque to the bench. Laser-engrave or etch all required lettering into these materials. The lettering must follow each surface perspective, share the scene lighting, and show subtle material reflections or pigment-filled recesses. The installation must feel like permanent school-garden signage, never like a presentation overlay.

Composition: medium-wide shot, robot and workbench centered, school and central tree softly visible behind, 40 mm cinematic lens, warm morning light, cyan rim reflection from the robot, accurate contact shadows, clean organized environment, sophisticated child-friendly 3D animation style.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the title on the main teaching-board frame; place the two section headings and all of their list items on two separate etched metal-and-glass panels; place the six process steps on six inlaid path markers; place the closing statement on a plaque fixed to the bench.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating typography, no subtitle, no lower third, no title overlay, no holographic interface, no digital speech bubble, no glowing words in the air, no extra text, no spelling errors, no detached metal plaque, no paper label pasted over the scene, no robot acting as a human replacement, no second robot, no people, no altered robot design, no dangerous tools, no sparks, no weapons, no clutter, no moved garden landmarks, no logos, no watermark.

teks:
Mini Project Example
A Little Robot Saves the School Garden
A little robot finds the school garden withering. It searches for the cause, fixes the sprinkler system, and helps the garden turn green again.
1
Finding the problem
2
Searching and fixing
3
Helping the garden turn green again
The child may change the characters, colors, setting, or storyline.

Create a cinematic 16:9 key art image for the mini project "A Little Robot Saves the School Garden". Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as strict visual references. Maintain the robot's exact face screen, eyes, white shell, navy joints, cyan core, violet accents, body proportions, hands, and feet. Maintain the exact school building, entrance gate, central tree, circular bench, raised beds, paths, fence, pipes, and tool board.

Show one coherent story moment with cause, action, and hope visible in a single environment. Little Robot kneels safely near a loosened irrigation connector beside one raised vegetable bed. One hand stabilizes the pipe and the other holds a small rounded wrench. A few plants nearest the damaged pipe are wilted, while healthy flowers farther away show what the garden can become again. A gentle test droplet appears at the repaired connector. The robot's cyan eyes communicate focused determination.

Integrate the complete presentation copy across believable physical story objects: a substantial carved teak project sign on the inside of the arched gate, a stone premise plaque beside the affected planting bed, three numbered garden-stage markers, and a final engraved statement on the gate threshold. Every surface must have realistic thickness, grain or stone texture, bolts or embedded mounting, bevels, sunlight, and contact shadows. The lettering must belong to each object rather than appearing as a movie title pasted over the image.

Composition: wide three-quarter garden view with the title sign readable in the foreground, robot repair action in the middle ground, school and central tree behind, 28–35 mm lens feeling, bright morning with slightly hopeful cinematic contrast, polished family-friendly 3D animation, readable silhouettes, detailed but uncluttered.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the slide title and project title on the main entrance sign; place the premise on a stone story plaque; place each numbered stage and its description on one of three physical garden markers; place the final change-permission statement on the gate threshold.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating title, no subtitle, no poster overlay, no text in the sky, no hologram, no speech bubbles, no extra words, no misspelling, no redesigned garden, no different school, no extra robots, no children, no dangerous machinery, no electrical sparks, no severe flooding, no dead garden, no weapon-like tools, no extra limbs or fingers, no deformed robot, no watermark, no logo, no cropped title sign.

teks:
3-Scene Story Structure
SCENE 1 · DISCOVERY
The Garden Withers
A little robot discovers the plants in the school garden are drying up and withering.
SCENE 2 · ACTION
The Robot Finds a Solution
The robot inspects the water pipes and tries to fix the sprinkler system.
SCENE 3 · RESOLUTION
The Garden is Green Again
Water flows again and the plants become fresh and colorful.

Create a polished 16:9 cinematic educational image showing a three-scene story structure as a real physical installation inside the established School Garden. Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as mandatory references. Keep the exact robot and environment identity consistent.

Build a permanent three-panel ceramic mosaic display into the low garden wall beside the main path. The display must be made from thick glazed tiles set into a real masonry frame with grout, bevels, sunlight, and contact shadows. Carve the required title into the top stone lintel. Beneath it, create three equal physical mosaic panels from left to right. Panel one depicts Little Robot discovering wilted plants; panel two depicts the same robot repairing the same irrigation pipe; panel three depicts the restored green garden with sprinklers. Fire each scene label, scene title, and scene description into its corresponding ceramic panel so the text is part of the glaze, never a caption placed on top.

Place the real Little Robot in front of the installation, gently pointing from the first panel toward the third with an open teaching gesture. The actual garden behind the display should subtly echo the same story location, with the affected bed and irrigation area recognizable.

Composition: medium-wide view at child eye level, display filling most of the frame while still grounded in the garden, 35–40 mm lens, clear left-to-right progression, warm bright-morning light, premium stylized 3D animation, realistic ceramic materials, crisp readable lettering, soft shadows, no clutter.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the title on the stone lintel; fire each scene label, scene title, and scene description into its corresponding one of the three ceramic mosaic panels.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating labels, no subtitle bar, no UI cards, no digital infographic, no arrow overlays, no holograms, no speech bubbles, no extra scene panels, no extra text, no misspelling, no repeated or inconsistent robot designs inside the mosaics, no detached frames, no cropped labels, no rearranged school garden, no people, no watermark, no logo, no illegible microtext, no photorealistic live action.

teks:
From Images to Storyboard
After all scene images are ready, AI can organize them into a clear 10-second video plan.
FINAL PLANNING OUTPUT
10-Second Storyboard
12 shots with visual directions for character movement, camera movement, composition, lighting, and effects.
ACTUAL AI WORKFLOW
PROMPT
Bikinin storyboard dengan tanda panah dan arti tanda panah tersebut untuk ini: video 10 detik
The storyboard connects finished images into one continuous visual sequence.
Arrows make movement and cinematic direction easier to understand before video generation.

Create a cinematic 16:9 educational 3D animation image showing how finished scene images become a storyboard. Use the uploaded Little Robot character sheet and School Garden environment sheet as mandatory references. Preserve the exact robot and garden production design.

Place a large physical storyboard board on the established wooden gardening workbench. The board must be a real framed object made from pale wood and cork, supported by sturdy feet, with believable thickness, pins, edge shadows, and sunlight. Pin twelve small printed frames to the board in a clean 4-by-3 grid. Every frame should depict a coherent progression in the same garden: establishing shot, robot entering, discovering wilted plants, repairing the pipe, water returning, plants recovering, garden transformation, robot satisfied, robot exiting, and final wide garden shot. Keep the robot identity and garden geography consistent across all printed frames. Use simple hand-drawn colored arrows printed or drawn directly onto the individual paper frames to indicate body movement, camera movement, composition, light, and effects.

Integrate all presentation copy into the physical storyboard workstation. Carve the title and introduction into the upper wooden frame, attach a mounted planning-output metal plaque, pin a real paper workflow-and-prompt card to the cork surface, and engrave both concluding statements into the wooden base. All typography must be physically printed, engraved, or carved into those objects with correct perspective, paper or material texture, sunlight, and contact shadows. Little Robot stands beside the board holding one paper frame and a red pencil, carefully placing the final frame into position.

Composition: medium-wide view, storyboard board dominant but fully visible, robot beside it, actual garden and school behind, 35 mm lens, bright morning light, premium family-friendly 3D animation, crisp material detail, readable physical header and plaque, organized visual hierarchy.

TEXT PLACEMENT: Place the title and introductory sentence on the storyboard-board frame; place the planning-output heading and all three planning-output lines on a mounted metal plaque; place the workflow heading, PROMPT label, and Indonesian prompt on a pinned paper card; place both closing statements on the wooden base.

NEGATIVE REQUIREMENTS: absolutely no readable or pseudo-readable text outside the authorized text list above; no invented title, tagline, subtitle, explanation, category, instruction, sequence label, plaque copy, book title, notebook writing, packet label, tool label, building sign, robot decal, camera branding, screen text, UI text, logo, watermark, random letters, gibberish, typographic decoration, or extra number; do not reproduce writing from reference images unless it is present verbatim in the authorized list; all unassigned surfaces, books, papers, screens, packages, tools, and props must remain completely blank and texture-only; no floating storyboard, no subtitle, no digital UI overlay, no text outside physical wood or metal surfaces, no giant hovering arrows, no holograms, no extra words, no misspelling, no random frame count, no inconsistent robot, no changing school architecture, no detached paper without pins, no illegible title, no people, no watermark, no logo, no cropped storyboard board, no duplicated limbs.
